name: Hoofd Communicatie / Director Corporate Communications
description: Heads of corporate communications, public affairs, or communications departments at medium to large organizations in the Netherlands — the leaders who own the communications function and are accountable for how it responds to AI.
painPoints: Leadership expects the communications team to have an AI position, but no one has defined what responsible AI adoption looks like for communications specifically
Team members are already using AI tools informally — without policy, oversight, or governance
Fear of reputational risk if AI-generated content goes wrong under their watch
Difficulty distinguishing which AI applications create real value from the noise of vendor claims
decisionCriteria: Advisors who understand communications from the inside — not technologists explaining AI to communicators
Practical, team-ready output: training, playbooks, governance frameworks that actually get used
No hype: honest about what AI can and cannot do for a communications team
Speed: the team needs to move from scattered adoption to governed practice without a 6-month transformation
skepticism: Has seen too many AI vendors promise simplicity and deliver complexity. Needs to see that AI4Comms understands the actual work of a communications team — not just AI capabilities.
adaptedMessage: Wij maken AI praktisch toepasbaar voor uw communicatieteam — met strategie, training en governance die past bij uw organisatie en uw risicoprofiel.

topObjection.objection: We kunnen dit zelf oppakken via onze IT- of digitale transformatie-afdeling.
topObjection.response: IT-afdelingen ontwerpen AI-governance voor systemen en data. Communicatie-governance vraagt iets anders: hoe beoordeel je AI-gegenereerde content op toon, reputatierisico en authenticiteit? Hoe train je een team om AI verantwoord te gebruiken voor persberichten, crisiscommunicatie of stakeholderbrieven? Dat vereist communicatie-expertise, niet alleen technische kennis.

name: Communicatieteam / Afdeling Corporate Communications
description: In-house communications teams and departments — the practitioners who do the daily work of corporate communications, media relations, content production, and stakeholder engagement, and who are directly affected by AI adoption.
painPoints: Already using AI tools informally but without guidance on what is responsible or effective
Unclear which tasks AI should and should not handle in a communications context
Concern about quality, accuracy, and brand voice when using AI-generated content
Feeling left behind by colleagues in other departments who seem to have adopted AI faster
decisionCriteria: Training that is relevant to communications work — not generic AI literacy
Practical tools: prompt playbooks, workflow guidance, approval checklists
Honest: what works, what doesn't, what the risks are
Respect for professional judgment: AI assists, the communicator decides
skepticism: Worried that AI adoption means less control over quality, or that it signals headcount pressure. Needs reassurance that AI augments the professional, not the other way around.
adaptedMessage: AI maakt jouw communicatiewerk sterker — als je het goed inricht. Wij leren je wat werkt, wat niet werkt, en hoe je het verantwoord toepast.

topObjection.objection: We have no time for training — we're already stretched.
topObjection.response: That is exactly the point: AI should reduce the load, not add to it. Training that is too abstract or too long does the opposite. Our programmes are designed around your actual workflows — short, targeted, and applied to the tasks your team already does.

name: Communicatiebureaus en -adviesbureaus
description: Communications agencies and consultancies serving clients in the Netherlands — firms that need to develop their own AI approach and advise their clients on AI in communications.
painPoints: Clients are asking about AI in communications — the agency needs a credible, grounded position
Staff are adopting AI tools informally; the agency needs governance and quality standards
Risk that AI-generated client deliverables create reputational or quality issues
Competitive pressure from agencies claiming AI superiority
decisionCriteria: Communications-specific AI advisory — not generic digital transformation consulting
Governance and policy frameworks the agency can adapt for its own use
Training that works for account teams at different seniority levels
A partner who can also help advise the agency's clients
skepticism: Agencies are cautious about bringing in a third party that might compete for their clients. Needs to see clear advisory positioning — AI4Comms advises the communications function, not the communications market.
adaptedMessage: Wij helpen communicatiebureaus een eerlijke, goed onderbouwde AI-aanpak te ontwikkelen — voor hun eigen processen en voor het advies aan hun klanten.

topObjection.objection: We already have our own AI tools and workflows.
topObjection.response: Having tools is not the same as having a governed, defensible AI practice. We work with agencies to define what good looks like: which AI applications are appropriate, how quality is maintained, how governance is documented, and how the agency can advise its own clients with confidence.
